жўқд»¶пјҡеӣһиҰҶвүҘ200еӯ— + и·Ё3+дёҚеҗҢдё»йЎҢзҡ„з¶ңеҗҲжҖ§еӣһзӯ”гҖӮзҹӯеӣһиҰҶ/ж“ҚдҪңзўәиӘҚдёҚеҠ гҖӮ

<observation>
<type>discovery</type>
<title>ai_multi_agent vs telegram-kiro-bridge жһ¶ж§Ӣе·®з•°еҲҶжһҗ</title>
<subtitle>Comparison report reveals fundamental differences in multi-agent topology, communication mechanisms, and feature sets between the two frameworks</subtitle>
<facts>
<fact>ai_multi_agent uses true multi-process architecture with Telegram Forum Topics (one topic per agent), while telegram-kiro-bridge uses single main agent with specialist subprocesses</fact>
<fact>ai_multi_agent agents reply via MCP tool calling reply() that posts to HTTP API :8470, while bridge uses streaming stdout with token protocol parsing</fact>
<fact>ai_multi_agent implements leader/worker roles with leader-only tools (send_to_instance, restart_team, create_forum_topic), bridge uses flat main/specialist model</fact>
<fact>telegram-kiro-bridge has 15+ token protocol types (ASK, SEND_FILE, SCHEDULE, RELAY_DELEGATE, PARALLEL_DELEGATE), ai_multi_agent has 9 MCP tools for inter-agent communication</fact>
<fact>bridge supports streaming chunk editing (real-time message updates), embedding routing (7 semantic routers), and cross-machine relay via Telegram+Redis; ai_multi_agent lacks these</fact>
<fact>ai_multi_agent has model/backend hot-swap via inline keyboard, built-in health monitor with 30s checks and auto-restart, warm pool subprocess preheating; bridge lacks hot-swap and warm pool</fact>
<fact>bridge implements night maintenance Dream pipeline (13 steps), wiki knowledge base with semantic search, reflexion+error classification, goal multi-turn continuation; ai_multi_agent lacks these</fact>
<fact>ai_multi_agent uses team.yaml centralized config and state/sessions.json atomic persistence, bridge scatters config across .env + specialist-domains.json + relay-peers.json</fact>
</facts>
<narrative>The comparison report (dated 2026-06-30) documents architectural philosophy differences: ai_multi_agent is "multiple agents forming a team, each with their role" (office with assigned desks metaphor) using Python + FastAPI HTTP daemon, while telegram-kiro-bridge is "one super bridge connecting all functions" (omnipotent secretary metaphor) using TypeScript + stdio JSON-RPC. Key technical divergences: (1) Communication - ai_multi_agent uses synchronous request_knowledge(60s timeout) and async send_to_instance() through HTTP, bridge uses token protocol with relay delegates; (2) Reply mechanism - ai_multi_agent's one-shot reply() MCP tool vs bridge's streaming chunk edit; (3) Agent isolation - ai_multi_agent's true multi-process with topic-based UI isolation vs bridge's manual /specialist switching; (4) Memory - ai_multi_agent relies on per-agent steering dirs, bridge has centralized MCP + sharding + embedding. Feature matrix shows bridge leads in user-facing capabilities (streaming, scheduling, goal continuation, wiki, 429 protection, dream maintenance) while ai_multi_agent leads in operational features (health monitor, warm pool, hot-swap, auto-restart via Task Scheduler). The report identifies bridge's main gaps as lack of hot-swap and session management, ai_multi_agent's gaps as missing streaming, embedding routing, and long-term memory infrastructure.</narrative>

<title>Comprehensive Architecture Comparison and Unique Feature Analysis</title>
<subtitle>Detailed technical comparison reveals complementary design philosophies with 10-dimension scoring and use case recommendations for each framework</subtitle>
<facts>
<fact>ai_multi_agent exclusive features: Forum Topic routing (zero-cost UI isolation per agent), MCP 3-layer injection strategy (disk fallback + ACP native + timing control), warm pool subprocess preheating, leader/worker role-based permissions, backend hot-swap via inline keyboard, ACP protocol JSONL trace per message</fact>
<fact>telegram-kiro-bridge exclusive features: streaming chunk edit with 429 protection, embedding router using local ONNX bge-small-zh-v1.5 for 7 semantic tasks (<2ms per query), cross-machine relay via Telegram bot-to-bot + Redis, goal multi-turn continuation across restarts, dream 13-step nightly pipeline (JSON-configurable), local LLM (Qwen3.6-35B-A3B MoE) for 7 accelerators with graceful fallback, token protocol 15+ types, self-improving agent with 14 error classifications</fact>
<fact>Scoring comparison (10-point scale): ai_multi_agent leads in multi-agent collaboration (8.5 vs 7.0), session management (8.5 vs 5.0), deployment ease (7.5 vs 5.5); bridge leads in memory/knowledge (9.5 vs 3.0), UX real-time (9.0 vs 5.0), self-improvement (9.0 vs 4.0), ops automation (9.5 vs 4.5), cross-machine capability (9.0 vs 1.0)</fact>
<fact>ai_multi_agent architecture flow: Telegram Forum Topic вҶ’ TelegramAdapter (topic_id routing) вҶ’ Daemon (lifecycle + health monitor 30s) вҶ’ Backend abstraction вҶ’ ACP SDK вҶ’ agent subprocess вҶ’ MCP tool reply() вҶ’ HTTP POST :8470 вҶ’ DaemonAPI вҶ’ back to Telegram</fact>
<fact>ai_multi_agent MCP injection uses 3-layer strategy: (1) disk write to agent-specific config (.kiro/settings/mcp.json for kiro/claude, opencode.json, .codex/config.toml), (2) ACP session/new native mcpServers parameter, (3) startup timing control (_remove_team_mcp before start вҶ’ API ready вҶ’ _inject_acp_mcp after)</fact>
<fact>ai_multi_agent dynamic injection generates system-overview.md per agent on every restart, ensuring latest rules: team member table, role definition (leader/worker), architecture diagram, communication rules, role-specific inject-rules (templates/inject-rules/all.md + leader.md/worker.md)</fact>
<fact>ai_multi_agent supports 4 backend types: kiro-cli (legacy stdin/stdout), acp:kiro (kiro-cli acp), acp:claude (claude-agent-acp), acp:codex (@zed-industries/codex-acp); all share AgentBackend ABC interface with start/send_message/stop/is_alive/is_ready methods</fact>
<fact>Recommendation split: ai_multi_agent suits team scenarios with multiple users per Topic, model/backend experimentation, beginner-friendly setup (team.yaml), session archive/resume, Python ecosystem; bridge suits single power-user, extreme UX (streaming + buttons), semantic memory accumulation, cross-machine collaboration, automated ops, local LLM cost savings, self-improving direction</fact>
<fact>Architecture philosophy: ai_multi_agent = "office with assigned desks" (centralized control, distributed execution), bridge = "omnipotent secretary calling specialists" (single entry point, extreme integration)</fact>
</facts>
<narrative>The comparison report (dated 2026-06-30) and architecture.md provide comprehensive technical analysis showing ai_multi_agent and telegram-kiro-bridge as complementary rather than competing frameworks. ai_multi_agent's unique strengths center on true multi-process agent isolation with Telegram Forum Topic-based UI separation (each agent = one topic, zero switching cost), sophisticated MCP injection using 3-layer strategy to prevent blocking (remove team entry before agent start вҶ’ wait for API ready вҶ’ inject via both disk and ACP protocol), warm pool for cold-start reduction, and leader/worker role hierarchy with leader-only tools (send_to_instance, create_forum_topic, restart_team). Its session management is mature with atomic JSON persistence, inline keyboard UI for switching/archiving/resuming, and auto-naming from ACP session_info_update events. Backend hot-swap lets users switch between kiro-cli/acp:claude/acp:codex via buttons without restart.

Bridge's unique strengths focus on UX and intelligence: streaming chunk editing creates "typing" effect by continuously updating the same Telegram message with 429 rate-limit protection and retry_after freezing; embedding router uses local ONNX model (bge-small-zh-v1.5) for 7 semantic routing tasks (memory recall, skill routing, wiki search, intent classification, sticker selection, notebook query, deduplication) with <2ms latency per turn; cross-machine relay enables agent collaboration across different machines via Telegram bot-to-bot + Redis with image return and loop guards; goal system provides multi-turn continuation with pause/resume/clear surviving restarts; dream pipeline automates 13 nightly maintenance steps (memorytoskill вҶ’ topicreview вҶ’ wikisync вҶ’ factlint вҶ’ wikilint вҶ’ skilllint вҶ’ ... вҶ’ backup вҶ’ restart) from JSON config; local LLM (Qwen3.6-35B-A3B MoE via llama.cpp) accelerates 7 tasks (topic classification, fact dedup, preamble compression, rerank) with graceful offline fallback; token protocol provides 15+ embedded commands (SEND_FILE, ASK, SCHEDULE, RESTART, WIKI_QUERY, SKILL_PROPOSE, SPECIALIST_PROXY, RELAY_DELEGATE, PARALLEL_DELEGATE) parsed from agent stdout; self-improving loop tracks 14 error types вҶ’ reflexion policy вҶ’ tool fail tracking вҶ’ capability gap detection вҶ’ auto-script generation.

The 10-dimension scoring reveals clear specialization patterns: ai_multi_agent excels at operational concerns (multi-agent 8.5, session mgmt 8.5, deployment 7.5, extensibility 8.0, code quality 8.0), while bridge dominates intelligence and automation (memory/knowledge 9.5, UX real-time 9.0, self-improvement 9.0, ops automation 9.5, cross-machine 9.0). Architecture docs confirm ai_multi_agent's central daemon manages all instances via FastAPI :8470 with health monitor (30s intervals, exponential backoff up to 10 retries), while bridge runs single main loop with specialist subprocesses. The dynamic injection mechanism in ai_multi_agent ensures every restart picks up latest team configuration and rules by regenerating system-overview.md (team member table marking "вҶҗ дҪ ", role definitions, architecture diagram, communication rules, role-specific templates), contrasting with bridge's static postinstall MCP write. Both frameworks reference each other in their docs, suggesting mutual learning: ai_multi_agent cites bridge's token protocol, embedding router, dream pipeline, relay, and environment portability (${MEMORY_DIR} variables) as inspirations worth studying.</narrative>
